notes.note: The Liberalia, festival of Liber (Bacchus) on 17 March, the day on which Roman boys traditionally took the toga virilis; here simply a calendar date.
The Quinquatrus, festival of Minerva on 19 March; Cicero plays on the coincidence of pleading Cornificius's case on Minerva's own day.

notes.anchor: our Minerva, guardian of the city, whom a whirlwind had toppled, should be restored
notes.note: A statue of Minerva (Minerva Custos Urbis) that Cicero himself had dedicated on the Capitol before going into exile in 58 BC; it had been thrown down by a storm, and the Senate now voted its restoration.

notes.anchor: the discomfiture of the Minotaur --- that is, of Calvisius and Taurus
notes.note: A learned pun: Minos plus Taurus yields the Minotaur, so Cicero compounds the names of the two opponents Calvisius and (Statilius) Taurus into the mythical monster.

notes.anchor: A complimentary decree was passed about you
notes.note: A senatus consultum honorificum, a formal resolution of the Senate commending Cornificius's conduct as governor of Africa.

notes.anchor: on the thirteenth day before the Kalends of January, laid the foundations of the state
notes.note: 20 December 44 BC, the date of Cicero's Third and Fourth Philippics, when he rallied the Senate and people against Antony; he reckons it the founding moment of the republican resistance.

notes.anchor: when the Etesian winds, like good citizens, refused to escort me
notes.note: The Etesians are the steady summer north winds of the Aegean; their being contrary to a southbound voyage to Greece let Cicero (jokingly crediting their patriotism) be driven back to Italy in 44 BC.

notes.anchor: carried me back to your fellow-tribesmen at Regium
notes.note: Regium (Rhegium, modern Reggio Calabria) on the toe of Italy; Cicero notes that its people shared Cornificius's voting-tribe.
Cicero quotes the comic playwright Terence (the phrase "this day brings a different life, demands different ways" echoes a line of his comedy), invoking literary authority for a change of conduct.

notes.anchor: embark with us --- and indeed to the stern
notes.note: The ship-of-state metaphor: the stern (puppis) is the helmsman's post, so Cicero invites Cornificius not merely aboard but to share in steering the republic.
